summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Ex Tempore Reasons – Interim orders – Where the father seeks to increase his time with the children from five nights per fortnight to week about – Application by the father seeking to travel overseas with the children to the United Kingdom and Country B – Where the parties' co-parenting relationship is poor – Orders previously made for a Family Report to be updated with appointments to occur in June 2026 – Where the Court considers it premature to make any change to the parenting arrangements pending the updated report – Application dismissed save and except for information sharing orders

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

KARI J:

INTRODUCTION

These are interlocutory parenting proceedings in relation to the parties' two children, X, born in 2019, who is six years of age, and Y, born in 2021, who is four years of age.
As permitted by s 69ZL of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”), I intend to give short form reasons this day in relation to the parenting dispute that is presently before the Court.
The dispute arises as a consequence of an Application in a Proceeding filed by the father on 10 March 2026. By that application, the father seeks a range of orders summarised as follows:
(a) an increase to the present time spending arrangements that he has with the children;

(b) orders providing for him to travel to the United Kingdom and Europe with the children in the June/July 2026 school holidays;

(c) orders loosely under the heading “Communication/non-denigration”, which provide not only for the parents to communicate with the children by telephone or video communication but, additionally, an injunction, which is the subject of agreement today;

(d) the father seeks access to the children's various school and H Family Services and/or child care records.

Additionally, there is a costs application, however, I make it plain to the parties today that I do not intend to determine any costs application. Necessarily, any determination of the same is informed by the orders to be made today.
The mother filed a Response to the Application in a Proceeding yesterday, being 23 March 2026. By that Response, the mother, essentially, asks the Court to dismiss the Application in a Proceeding filed by the father and that an order for costs be made.
The proceedings have come on with relative swiftness before the Court in circumstances where the Court had capacity to hear the application as a consequence of a trial having been vacated in my docket. The parties should not understand that that is a usual occurrence, but simply a feature of the Court having availability at this juncture.

LEGAL PRINCIPLES

In any parenting proceedings before the Court, the legal principles are settled and well defined. They are contained in Pt VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), and those provisions provide that the Court has an obligation to make orders which regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration as set out in s 60CA of the Act.
In determining what orders are considered to be in a child's best interests, the Court must have regard to those factors set out in s 60CC of the Act. Section 60CC(1) sets out six separate factors to be considered under the heading “General Considerations”, and in particular, s 60CC(2)(a) provides that the Court must consider any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child, and any family violence order that applies or has applied to a member of the child's family.
The current application has not occurred in a vacuum. Some background facts are necessary to discuss at this juncture.

BRIEF BACKGROUND

The children's parents were in a relationship and resided together from approximately March 2017. There appears to be some dispute about the duration of the living together period in circumstances where the parties met in May 2016 in the United States of America, where the mother was living at that time. The mother says that she travelled to Australia to live with the father in March 2017, whereas the father asserts that she travelled to visit him at that juncture. Nothing at this juncture turns on that dispute between the parties. What is agreed by the parties is that by August 2017, the mother had obtained a sponsored visa to live in Australia and that in July 2018, the father asserts the parties became engaged.
In 2019, the parties moved from Melbourne, where they had been living, to South Australia to take up residence, ultimately, in a property that the father purchased in Suburb G. After moving to South Australia, both of the children were born. In March 2021, the mother obtained a visa to remain living in Australia.
There is equally a dispute as to the time at which the parties separated. Again, for present purposes, nothing turns on that dispute but I note that the father asserts the date of separation to be some time in June 2023, whereas the mother asserts the date to be 31 August 2023.
It is the father's position that, following separation, the parties agreed that the children ought live in some form of shared care arrangement. That is not a topic that the Court is able to determine at this juncture. What is clear, however, is that shortly after separation, a living arrangement which saw the children live with the father for five nights each fortnight and otherwise in the mother's primary care was implemented between the parties. The five night a fortnight regime was split across a two week cycle.
These proceedings were commenced by the mother on 8 December 2023, when she did so, despite the application indicating that parenting orders were sought, no such orders were sought. Ultimately, however, parenting proceedings were joined, the proceedings having commenced with a focus as to property and financial issues as and between the parties and, in particular, from the mother's perspective, urgent and interim arrangements with respect to spousal maintenance.
The parties have been before the Court a number of times in relation to a range of interlocutory issues. Those issues have included matters pertaining to the progression of the financial proceedings, including issues relating to the mother's financial support, but, also, issues relating to the parenting arrangements for the children.
I do not propose at this juncture to detail that which has occurred in relation to the financial proceedings, other than my observation that it is apparent to the Court that all issues, including the financial issues in these proceedings have been the subject of litigation and seeming acrimony between the parties.
On 5 June 2024, significantly, orders were made by consent by a senior judicial registrar that the parties participate in and facilitate the children's participation in a Family Report. That report was ultimately prepared by Ms F, with appointments having taken place in September 2024 and the report being released and dated 24 October 2024. I have had regard to Ms F's report for present purposes, and I shall come back to it later in these reasons.
To the parties' credit, following the release of Ms F's report, the proceedings were scheduled for an interlocutory hearing before a senior judicial registrar on 6 November 2024. I say to the parties' credit because at that hearing, the Court understands that orders were ultimately made by consent between the parties as to a range of matters, save and except as to a determination in relation to the schooling arrangements for the children.
The orders made on 6 November 2024, relevantly, provided as follows in summary:
(a) Firstly, that the children live with the mother.

(b) Secondly, that the children spend five nights per fortnight with the father as follows:

(i) In week 1 from the conclusion of school, ELC or child care on Monday until the commencement of school, ELC or childcare on Tuesday, together with time from the conclusion of school, ELC or child care on Friday until the commencement of school, ELC or child care on Monday.

(ii) In week 2, the children spend time with the father from the conclusion of school, ELC or child care on Thursday until the commencement of school, ELC or child care on Friday.

Significantly, from the father’s perspective today, orders were also made by consent providing for the mother to travel with the children to the United States of America from late December 2024 until mid-January 2025. Orders were also made providing for the children’s time spending with each parent over Christmas of 2024 and during Easter. Arrangements were made for handovers of the children and, in addition, orders were made in relation to the mother's intended, at that stage, overseas travel. The children's names were otherwise removed from the Airport Watchlist to the extent that it was necessary to do so.
I do not propose at this juncture to set out the education arrangements, which were the subject of judicial determination that day, other than to note that orders were made for the children to attend upon a range of educational/child care facilities, and all three of those facilities form part of the orders sought by the father today, so far as the release of information to him from each of those facilities are concerned.

THE CURRENT APPLICATION

As indicated earlier, the father has now filed an Application in a Proceeding which has ostensibly at its focus a progression of his time spending with the children, together with orders for international travel.

DISCUSSION

As discussed by Christie J in Kennedy & Peyton (No 3) [2025] FedCFamC1F 167 (“Kennedy & Peyton (No 3)”) at [26]–[31], in which she cited the decision of Austin and Williams JJ in Radecki & Radecki [2024] FedCFamC1A 246; (2024) FLC 94-232 at [36]:
Orders made in parenting proceedings are never unequivocally final, because the Court is empowered by s 65D(2) of the Act, to discharge, vary, suspend or revive some or all parenting orders. Parenting orders can be varied by consent of the parties or by a court determination. Parenting orders are only “final” in so far as they determine an existing cause of action brought under Pt VIII of the Act.
As Christie J pointed out, the Court will vary orders where the evidence establishes that such a variation is in the best interests of the children, stating in Kennedy & Peyton (No 3) at [29]:
While the provisions of s 65DAAA of the Act do not apply to interlocutory parenting orders of the Court, it is necessary for the Court to highlight that interlocutory hearings are a summary form of hearing and usually best utilised when the circumstances warrant that is, in circumstances of urgency and where the untested evidence supports the necessity to revisit existing interlocutory orders.
As her Honour went on to discuss, the recent amendments to Pt VII of the Act are such that the pathway identified by the Full Court in Goode & Goode [2006] FamCA 958; (2006) FLC 93-296 as to how interim proceedings ought be conducted remains applicable despite the recent amendments. That was set out as four steps identified at [82] in Goode & Goode [2006] FamCA 958; (2006) FLC 93-296 in the following terms:
82. In an interim case that would involve the following:
(a) identifying the competing proposals of the parties;

(b) identifying the issues in dispute in the interim hearing;

(c) identifying any agreed or uncontested relevant facts;
(d) considering the matters set out in section 60CC that are relevant and, if possible, making findings about them (in interim proceedings there may be little uncontested evidence to enable more than a limited consideration of these matters to take place);
I propose to adopt that pathway throughout these reasons.
I have already identified the competing proposals of the parties.
The issues in dispute today are clear. The father promotes a range of orders and the mother, bar one topic, does not consent to them.
Turning to any agreed or uncontested facts, it appears that it is not in dispute that the children have a good relationship with both of their parents. Indeed, this is a matter that Ms F commented upon when she prepared her Family Report in October 2024. At paragraph 118 of the Family Report, Ms F identified that both the parties and collateral sources of information do not dispute that both children appeared, at that juncture, to be “thriving”.
There were, however, a number of issues discussed in Ms F's report which were then the subject of dispute between the parties and remain the subject of dispute today.
In particular, Ms F commented at paragraph 119 of the Family Report that the parties' co-parenting relationship is poor. On the state of the evidence presently before the Court today, it is apparent, at least at this interlocutory stage, that that poor co-parenting relationship has continued.
In addition, an issue in dispute between the parties relates to the mother's allegations that the father exercised and subjected her to family violence in the form of financial control. Whilst that topic is the subject of controversy between the parties and, clearly, is likely to be a trial issue between the parties, Ms F then, and the Court now, cannot overlook the allegations that have been made, nor is the Court in a position to dismiss those allegations without further enquiry.
Indeed, it was apparent to Ms F at the time of the preparation of her Family Report that the financial dispute between the parties was having an impact on the parenting arrangements. As I say, the extent to which the Court ultimately forms that view is a matter for determination if these proceedings progress to a final hearing.
Having said that, those matters are not insignificant in terms of the dispute that exists between the parties today. At paragraph 129 of the Family Report, Ms F identified her concerns that the father will, over time, engage in behaviours that will undermine or negatively impact the children's relationship with the mother or his capacity to quarantine his emotions from the children will reduce.
Ms F commented that it already appears to be clouding the father's capacity to be child focused in his thinking, considering his explanations for X’s distress on the day of the assessment, and accordingly recommendations were made for the father to engage in some counselling. The father indicates to the Court that he has undertaken counselling as recommended by Ms F. There is, however, at this stage, no evidence before the Court in relation to that counselling. While I take the father at his word that he has engaged in that counselling, what is not known is any detail in relation to that counselling, what it has entailed and how the father has presented, reacted and adapted to the issues raised in counselling.
Equally, the Court does not know the focus of that counselling, and in the absence of that information, which would ordinarily come before the Court in the form of a report, the Court is no better informed today than Ms F was when she prepared her report in relation to those concerns that she articulated.
The Court acknowledges that Ms F raised some concern at paragraph 122 of the Family Report as to certain aspects of each of the parties' narrative and what that might mean for their respective reliability and motivations concerning the parenting dispute, but also likely as to their reliability and motivations concerning the financial dispute.
Ms F commented that the financial dispute was having an impact on the parenting arrangements for the children. Indeed, she commented at paragraph 123 of the Family Report, that the notion of brokering the children's time so as to elicit increased financial support was something that had been raised with her, with her concluding that it seems highly at odds with the conveyance of being fearful for one's life, so far as the allegations that have been made by the mother. All of those matters are relevant from the Court's perspective.
On 13 August 2025, orders were made by consent before a senior judicial registrar for the preparation of an updated Family Report by Ms F. The Court understands, and it is an agreed fact today, that appointments have now been scheduled with Ms F for a report to be prepared, with those appointments to take place on or about 11 June 2026. The mother makes a criticism of the father that in the shadow of those appointments having been arranged, some six days later, the father filed the present Application in a Proceeding.
Irrespective of the mother's criticism in that regard, as raised with the father during the course of his submissions today, the Court has some significant concerns as to the vacuum in evidence presently before the Court, as to a range of matters that are relevant in the Court's consideration, frankly, to all of the issues before the Court today.
Whilst the Court accepts that it is not bound by the recommendations of any report writer, necessarily, the expert evidence of the report writer is a relevant and useful piece of evidence for the Court, particularly at an interlocutory stage. Such evidence is also relevant at the stage of a final hearing, the difference, however, being at that juncture that there is the ability to test the opinions of the report writer.
At an interlocutory stage, where, as I have earlier indicated, the process for any hearing is an abbreviated process, the Court is not able to determine factual disputes, nor, frankly, disputes about the weight to be attributed to the opinions of any expert. Necessarily, it is difficult for the Court to ignore at an interlocutory stage the recommendations of any expert.
It is of some concern to the Court that there has been no update from Ms F since her report of 24 October 2024. As discussed with the father during the course of his submissions, that there is a report very soon to be prepared raises immediate concerns from the Court's perspective as to prematurely making any changes to the children's parenting arrangements when it is not known what, if any, recommendations Ms F might ultimately make when she prepares her updated report.
Those recommendations may be that the current parenting arrangements ought continue. It may be that there should be an increase to time spending. It may equally be that Ms F recommends some change or reduction to the time spending the father is currently having with the children, albeit I accept that on the state of the evidence presently before the Court, the latter would appear unlikely but not out of the realms of possibility, that Ms F would recommend any such reduction.
The absence of evidence from Ms F is a significant hurdle so far as the main issues the father presses before the Court today, regarding an increase to his time spending, together with the proposed overseas travel.
The father, however, ought understand that interlocutory hearings are not like final hearings. It may be that at some juncture in the future, once Ms F's report has been received, there is some merit in revisiting the parenting arrangements for these children on an interlocutory basis. I do not, however, in the absence of Ms F's updated Family Report, consider that it is appropriate at this juncture to revisit those parenting arrangements. That is not to say that I have formed any concluded view as to whether, ultimately, those interlocutory arrangements should prevail following the release of Ms F's report, nor that they should be the arrangements that will ultimately be those implemented by the Court if asked to adjudicate the final parenting orders for these children.
What I make clear to the parties is that in the absence of Ms F's report, pre-emptively considering the children's parenting arrangements is a difficulty because there is an evidentiary lacuna.
It is not lost on the Court that the children are now significantly older than they were when the first report was prepared. Having said that, however, they are still very young children. Importantly, it is an agreed fact that the children, as a routine arrangement, have never spent more than three nights in a block away from the primary care of the mother.
That factor is of significance when the Court is turning its mind to the proposed overseas travel. I make it clear that consideration of the proposed overseas travel is finely balanced. It is finely balanced because the father, quite properly, wishes to enjoy holiday time with the children overseas, and it must be said that the enjoyment of overseas travel that these children are fortunate enough to experience with their parents generally would be considered to be in the children's best interests. That they would have the ability to spend time with the paternal family, both in the United Kingdom and on a family holiday to Country B, generally would be considered to be in these children's best interests.
Speaking against those arrangements and proposals, however, and leaving aside the amount of time that the children would be away from the mother, is the current global uncertainty that has arisen in relation to the crisis in the Middle East and the concomitant impact that crisis is having on international travel arrangements more generally. I accept that on the face of the evidence presently before the Court, that it does not appear that the father is a flight risk and there are no concerns at this juncture that if the travel was to be permitted, that the father would not return the children to Australia.
Having said all of that however, I make it clear that in the absence of an updated Family Report and opinion from Ms F, which should and could include her opinion as to overseas travel, the Court is reluctant to make those orders today.
Again, I make it clear that that is not a final determination in relation to the topic of overseas travel. It is simply a determination of the present application that is before the Court.
For all of those reasons, I do not propose to make the orders as sought by the father in relation to the change to the current time spending arrangements provided for in paragraphs 1 to 6 of his Application in a Proceeding, nor those orders set out in paragraphs 7 to 13 in relation to the proposed overseas travel in the June/July 2026 school holiday period.
In relation to the proposed orders 14 and 15 regarding communication, I do not consider at this stage it necessary for there to be orders providing for communication as proposed by the father. I have come to that view in circumstances where the current parenting arrangements see the father spending substantial and significant time with the children, and given the way in which the time is spread across a fortnight period, there is never, as he conceded during the course of his submissions, a long period of time that he does not see the children. I also do not consider it appropriate to make those orders because I consider the same to likely be a further point of disputation between the parties.
As indicated earlier, the mother agrees to the order proposed in paragraph 16 of the father’s Application in a Proceeding, being the injunctive order, and I shall make that order by consent.
In relation to the orders sought at paragraphs 17 and 18 of the father's Application in a Proceeding, relating to the provision and access to records from the children's H Family Services and child care facilities, it is somewhat surprising that such an application needs to be made in circumstances where both parties have an obligation for disclosure.
It is the father's position that the mother is not forthcoming about a whole range of arrangements she makes in relation to the H Family Services and child care services that she engages for the children. The Court is told that the father has issued subpoenas in relation to obtaining various records. Clearly, some of the records, if not all of the records, that the father seeks to obtain are of direct relevance not only to the parenting dispute between the parties but also the financial issues pending before the Court.
Whilst the Court understands that the mother opposes the orders pursued in that regard by the father, given that all of the information is, on its face, relevant and discoverable, I propose to make orders, not quite as the father proposes, but which provide for the release of information directly to the father from each of those facilities.
I otherwise, as earlier indicated, do not today propose to hear submissions in relation to costs but I will reserve the parties' respective costs arising from those applications before the Court. I will not, however, deal with costs separately, and they will be reserved to the final hearing.
NOTE:

These reasons were delivered ex tempore and have been settled from the transcript. Grammatical errors have been corrected, citations have been added and amendments have been made to make the orally delivered reasons clearer and easier to read.
